title: The Two Worlds of John Dee
subtitle: From Mortlake's library to Rudolfine Bohemia and back, 1527–1609
subject: john-dee
design_note: Modern Atlas (with Noir one click away): Dee's life splits into daylight and shadow — court mathematician and angel-conjuror. The map opens in the daylight register; switch to Noir at Kraków and watch the same route change meaning.
provenance_note: Itinerary after Glyn Parry, The Arch-Conjuror of England (2011); Peter French, John Dee: The World of an Elizabethan Magus (1972); Dee's own diaries (ed. Fenton). Dee's continental years are unusually well documented by his diary; the poverty of the final years is attested but sparsely dated.

stops.what: Born to a Welsh mercer attached to Henry VIII's court — close enough to power to be formed by it, never close enough to be secure in it.
St John's College, then founding fellow of Trinity: eighteen-hour study days, and a stage machine for Aristophanes so convincing it earned his first reputation as a conjuror.
Studies with Gemma Frisius and Gerardus Mercator — the best mathematical geography in Europe. Comes home carrying globes and instruments England had never seen.
Lectures on Euclid to an overflowing hall at the Collège de Rheims, aged twenty-three — the celebrity moment he spent the rest of his life trying to recover.
Service under Edward, arrest under Mary — charged with 'calculing' the queen's nativity, 1555 — then astrologer to the new Queen Elizabeth, electing her coronation date.

Builds England's greatest private library — some 4,000 volumes. Writes the Monas Hieroglyphica's afterlives, advises voyages of discovery, coins 'British Empire'. Then Edward Kelley arrives with a scrying stone.
Departs England with the Polish prince Olbracht Łaski, family and angels in tow. In Kraków the 'actions' with Kelley intensify: the angelic language dictated table by table.
Audience with Rudolf II: Dee exhorts the emperor to repentance on angelic instruction. The papal nuncio presses for his expulsion; the two magi are banished from imperial lands.

Refuge with Vilém of Rožmberk. Kelley's alchemical star rises past Dee's; the notorious 'cross-matching' of wives is recorded in the diary in Dee's own appalled hand. They part: Kelley to imprisonment and death, Dee to England.
Returns to find the great library pillaged by former friends. Petitions the queen for redress and a post; receives sympathy and very little else.

Warden of Christ's College, Manchester — a fractious northern posting that feels like exile. Consulted on demonic possession; quarrels with the fellows; buries his wife and children in the plague of 1605.
Dies poor at Mortlake in the winter of 1608/9, selling books to eat, still petitioning to be cleared of the name of conjuror. Ashmole later gathers what remains.
stops.source: Parry 2011, epilogue (burial date approximate — parish register lost)
